initiative_name: ## Action Area 1: Improve Energy Efficiency of Buildings And Key Infrastructure
parent_statement: Improve energy efficiency of buildings and key infrastructure through a package of priority actions (green building standards, low-income efficiency, outreach/education, LED streetlights, and residential retrofit financing).
coverage_assessment: The sub-actions collectively address major efficiency levers across municipal buildings (LEED/Net Zero), low-income housing retrofits, community-wide consumption reduction via outreach, streetlight retrofits, and residential retrofit financing tied to equipment/appliance upgrades. A remaining gap is that the green building standards language is explicitly municipal-focused; broader private-sector building code upgrades are not clearly stated in the verbatim text, so coverage of “all buildings” is not complete.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary and non-contradictory: standards reduce new/renovation loads, programs and financing drive retrofits, outreach targets behavior and operations, and LED streetlights deliver a clear infrastructure upgrade. Timelines are generally consistent (near-term 2022/2025 actions feeding into longer-term 2030 completion), with no apparent conflicts.

strong_signals: Multiple explicit numeric targets (100% compliance by 2025, 500% increase over 10 years, 30% per-household reduction, ≥10% consumption reduction objective, replace all non-LED by 2030 starting 2025, 15% of residences by 2025), concrete retrofits (LED streetlights, weatherization/retrofits), clear near- and medium-term milestones (2022/2025/2030)
initiative_reasoning: The initiative aims to cut energy use in buildings and key infrastructure through standards, retrofit delivery (including low-income), financing mechanisms for residential upgrades, outreach-driven reductions, and a full LED streetlight conversion. Sub-action strengths: 4 strong (Actions 1, 2, 4, 5), 0 moderate, 1 weak (Action 3 lacks a timeline and is process-oriented). The parent header does not add a binding legal mechanism or explicit spatial scope, so weaker items are not materially strengthened by inheritance; however, the cluster still contains ample measurable, concrete commitments. Coverage is strong because it spans municipal buildings, residential upgrades, low-income programs, and public lighting, though it does not fully specify broader private-sector code requirements beyond municipal facilities. Coherence is high because the components reinforce one another (standards + financing + retrofit programs + infrastructure upgrade + outreach) and timelines are aligned. Applying the initiative soundness rule: coverage_score (0.78) ≥ 0.6, coherence_score (0.90) ≥ 0.5, aggregate_measurability = Yes, at least half of sub-actions are strong/moderate (4/5), and at least one is strong (several). Therefore the initiative is SOUND, and with confidence 0.84 the final verdict is True.

initiative_name: Priority Action W.6 Enhance Methane Recovery and Landfill Gas Destruction

The only notable gap is that “promote greater methane recovery over biogas flaring” is not translated into a specific measurable requirement or target beyond feasibility evaluation at WWTPs.
coherence_assessment: The sub-actions are complementary: assessment/feasibility work (starting 2020) leads into implementation and monitoring plans, alongside parallel tracks for landfills and WWTPs. The legislation item functions as a cross-cutting enabling mechanism rather than a duplicate, and there are no apparent internal contradictions, though Action 1’s 2020 target is very near-term relative to “starting in 2020” assessment.

strong_signals: 75% to 85% landfill biogas capture rate by 2020, targeted completion by 2035 for Class III/IV controls, install methane recovery systems in all WWTP facilities in Shelby County by end of 2035, implementation and monitoring plan
initiative_reasoning: The initiative aims to reduce methane emissions by improving landfill gas capture (Class I from 75% to 85% by 2020), extending biogas controls to Class III/IV landfills (completion by 2035), and installing methane recovery at all Shelby County WWTPs by 2035, with monitoring/implementation plans. Sub-action strength mix: 2 strong (Actions 1 and 4), 2 moderate (Actions 2 and 3), 0 weak—so at least half are strong/moderate and at least one is strong. The parent establishes spatial/institutional scope via specific facility classes and “all facilities within Shelby County,” but does not itself establish an enforceable binding mechanism (Action 3 proposes legislation rather than citing an existing ordinance). Coverage is high because the subs directly map to each element of the objective; the main gap is that the ‘promote methane recovery over flaring’ element is not operationalized with a numeric requirement. Coherence is solid: early assessment/feasibility beginning in 2020 supports later installations and monitoring, and legislation is a complementary enabler. Applying the soundness rule: coverage_score (0.8) ≥ 0.6, coherence_score (0.75) ≥ 0.5, aggregate_measurability is Yes, ≥ half subs are strong/moderate, and at least one is strong → SOUND; with confidence 0.78 the final verdict is True.
